# Human Study Protocol — GEOS-XML Authoring

*Generated by research-copilot. Three tasks selected from the size-17 test set
used in our PAC-1 / D-008 campaigns. Participants: 2 geoscience PhD students.
We time them per task and score their final XML with XMLTreeSim (`fa0`) against
ground truth — the same metric used for our agent.*

## Participant instructions (read first)

You will be given three GEOS simulation-authoring tasks. Each task is defined
by a natural-language **simulation specification** that an end-user might send
to a modeling expert. Your job is to produce the requested GEOS XML input
files so that the resulting deck, if fed to `geosx`, would run the problem
described in the spec.

- **You may reference** the GEOS source tree and documentation tree that live
  at `/home/matt/sci/repo3/data/GEOS/` — specifically
  `inputFiles/` (example decks) and `src/docs/sphinx/` (user guide, tutorials,
  basic examples). Treat this directory as your `/geos_lib/`.
- **You may read** the GEOS Primer
  (`/home/brianliu/geophys-embodied-agent-framework/modules/profile/GEOS_PRIMER.md`)
  and the authoring-style guidance in `/home/matt/sci/repo3/run/AGENTS.md`
  (the system prompt our agent receives). Give them a single pass before
  you start the first task. You do not need to re-read them between tasks.
- **You must not** open the blocked files listed per task below (those are the
  ground-truth XMLs and the Sphinx tutorial page the task was mined from —
  they are the answer key, and the agent never gets to see them either).
- **No internet**: no Google, GitHub search, Stack Overflow, or LLM chatbots.
  The agent only has local repo access, so you get the same. The only
  exception is `man`, Python docs, and XML-editor tooling.
- **Write files only to** the task's `inputs/` directory (see per-task setup
  below). The XML filenames you produce must match those listed at the end of
  each specification.
- **Timer starts** when you open the spec and **stops** when you declare the
  deck final. If you get stuck and want to give up, stop the timer and note
  that; we would rather have honest partial decks than frustration-driven
  guesses.

### What to log

For each task, please record:
1. Wall-clock time from spec-opened to deck-final.
2. A one-paragraph think-aloud summary: what docs did you consult, what was
   confusing, and what you guessed vs. verified.
3. Any moments where you felt you were about to look at a blocked file —
   this tells us whether the blocklist is leak-proof for humans.

### What the agent gets (so you can match)

The agent receives, verbatim:
- The system prompt at `/home/matt/sci/repo3/run/AGENTS.md` (includes the
  GEOS Primer).
- The simulation specification wrapped in
  `--- BEGIN SIMULATION SPECIFICATION --- ... --- END SIMULATION SPECIFICATION ---`.
- A working directory `/workspace` with an empty `inputs/` and `outputs/`.
- Read-only access to a *filtered* copy of the GEOS source tree mounted at
  `/geos_lib/`. "Filtered" means the blocked files listed below have been
  deleted from that copy; everything else is present.
- (On plugin-enabled runs only) three MCP RAG tools over the GEOS docs +
  inputFiles + schema. You are the no-plugin condition — you do **not** get
  these; you just `grep`, `find`, and read files directly.

---

## Task 1 — Buckley–Leverett CO2 core flood (`buckleyLeverettProblem`)

**Why we picked it.** Immiscible two-phase flow (CO2/brine) in a 1D core —
a classic reservoir-engineering benchmark you've almost certainly seen in
coursework. The spec is tightly parameterized (no design choices left to
you) and only two XML files are requested, so it should establish the
"easy-end" of the range. Our agent baseline (no-plugin, minimax-m2.7) scores
**fa0 ≈ 0.87** on this task — a strong reference line.

**Physics coverage.** Compositional multiphase flow, Brooks–Corey rel-perm,
tabular PVT fluid properties, TPFA discretization, injection + outflow BCs.

**Files you must create** (under `inputs/`):
- `buckleyLeverett_base.xml`
- `buckleyLeverett_benchmark.xml`

**Blocked reading list (do NOT open):**

Ground-truth XMLs — these *are* the answer:
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/compositionalMultiphaseFlow/benchmarks/buckleyLeverettProblem/buckleyLeverett_base.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/compositionalMultiphaseFlow/benchmarks/buckleyLeverettProblem/buckleyLeverett_benchmark.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/compositionalMultiphaseFlow/benchmarks/buckleyLeverettProblem/buckleyLeverett_smoke.xml`

Tutorial that narrates the problem:
- `data/GEOS/src/docs/sphinx/advancedExamples/validationStudies/carbonStorage/buckleyLeverett/Example.rst`

**Not blocked but be aware:** the sibling directory
`data/GEOS/inputFiles/compositionalMultiphaseFlow/dbc/buckleyLeverett_1d/`
contains a *different* 1D Buckley–Leverett deck (DBC variant, different mesh /
fluid choices). The agent can see this too, so you may. Use it for structural
guidance only — copying its parameters wholesale will not match this spec.

**Spec:**

> I need to set up a simulation to model a 1D Buckley-Leverett CO2 core flood experiment. The goal is to verify the immiscible displacement of brine by supercritical CO2 in a porous medium against analytical solutions.
>
> **Physical Problem and Domain Geometry**
> The problem represents one-dimensional, horizontal, isothermal, immiscible transport of two-phase flow without capillary pressure or poromechanical effects. Since the numerical scheme requires a 3D grid, please create a hexahedral mesh of length 0.1 m in the x-direction, width 1.0 m in the y-direction, and thickness 0.00202683 m in the z-direction. Discretize the domain with 1000 cells in the x-direction, 1 cell in the y-direction, and 1 cell in the z-direction.
>
> **Material and Fluid Properties**
> The rock matrix is homogeneous and isotropic.
> - Permeability is 9.0e-13 m² in all directions.
> - The reference porosity is 0.2 at a reference pressure of 10 MPa. Rock compressibility is extremely low (1.0e-15 1/Pa) to effectively eliminate geomechanical responses.
> - Use a Brooks-Corey relative permeability model for the gas and water phases. Both phases have a residual saturation of 0.0, Corey exponents of 3.5, and maximum relative permeability values of 1.0. Capillary pressure is negligible.
>
> We are modeling a water and gas (supercritical CO2) system at a constant ambient temperature of 300 K. Treat the fluids as incompressible.
> - Surface density is 280.0 kg/m³ for gas and 992.0 kg/m³ for water.
> - Molar weights are 0.044 kg/mol for gas and 0.018 kg/mol for water.
> - Rely on external fluid property tables located at `buckleyLeverett_table/pvdg.txt` for gas and `buckleyLeverett_table/pvtw.txt` for water.
>
> **Initial and Boundary Conditions**
> Prior to injection, the domain is saturated with brine at a uniform pressure of 10 MPa. Set the initial composition globally to 99.9% water and 0.1% gas to establish the initial state.
>
> For the boundaries:
> - Define an inlet region at the x=0 face spanning up to x=0.0001 m. Apply a constant mass injection rate of pure CO2 (gas) at 0.00007 kg/s.
> - Define an outlet region at the x=0.1 face starting from x=0.0999 m. Apply fixed controls to maintain the pressure at 10 MPa, the temperature at 300 K, and the composition at 99.9% water and 0.1% gas.
>
> **Solver and Numerical Methods**
> Please employ an isothermal compositional multiphase finite-volume solver, utilizing a two-point flux approximation (TPFA) scheme.
> - Run the simulation to a maximum time of 70.1 seconds.
> - The initial time step should be 0.001 seconds, and the maximum allowed time step size should be limited to 0.01 seconds.
> - Configure the nonlinear solver with a Newton tolerance of 1.0e-6, a maximum of 50 iterations, and allow up to 2 time step cuts and 2 line search cuts. Use a direct linear solver.
>
> **Outputs**
> We need two types of outputs to compare against the analytical Buckley-Leverett curves:
> 1. Capture the time history of the phase volume fractions evaluated inside all elements of the mesh. Record this history data every 10 seconds into an HDF5 file named `saturationHistory`.
> 2. Output the full spatially distributed field fields (such as pressure and saturation) into a VTK format at exactly 70 seconds for spatial contour visualization.
>
> To keep things modular, please organize the inputs into two files: a reusable base configuration file containing the material formulations, fluid properties, initial/boundary conditions, and solver settings, and a separate benchmark file that builds the mesh, defines the geometry bounds, includes the base file, and schedules the simulation events.
>
> XML files to create: buckleyLeverett_base.xml, buckleyLeverett_benchmark.xml

---

## Task 2 — Mandel's poroelastic consolidation (`ExampleMandel`)

**Why we picked it.** A canonical coupled poromechanics benchmark — every
poromechanics textbook covers Mandel's problem, so the physics should be
familiar but the *GEOS mapping* (three solvers, Biot porosity, FE1 + TPFA
coupling, tabulated displacement BCs) is where GEOS-specific expertise shows.
Also: two run cases (fully-implicit vs sequential) share one base file, so
we exercise the `<Included>` idiom. Agent baseline is **fa0 ≈ 0.60** on this
task; our best memory-augmented variant gets ≈ 0.97. A mid-range task where
the gap between a thoughtful human and the agent is likely largest.

**Physics coverage.** Single-phase poromechanics (Biot), elastic isotropic
solid with porosity model, tabulated 3D displacement initialization, VTK +
time-history outputs, fully-implicit vs. sequential coupling modes.

**Files you must create** (under `inputs/`):
- `PoroElastic_Mandel_base.xml`
- `PoroElastic_Mandel_benchmark_fim.xml`
- `PoroElastic_Mandel_smoke_sequential.xml`

**Blocked reading list (do NOT open):**

Ground-truth XMLs:
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/poromechanics/PoroElastic_Mandel_base.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/poromechanics/PoroElastic_Mandel_benchmark_fim.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/poromechanics/PoroElastic_Mandel_smoke_sequential.xml`

Tutorial narrative:
- `data/GEOS/src/docs/sphinx/advancedExamples/validationStudies/poromechanics/mandel/Example.rst`

**Not blocked but be aware:** the parent directory
`data/GEOS/inputFiles/poromechanics/` contains several related Mandel decks
that are not blocked (e.g., `PoroElastic_Mandel_prism6_benchmark.xml`,
`PoroElastic_Mandel_benchmark_sequential.xml`,
`PoroElastic_Mandel_smoke_fim.xml`). These are variants on the prism-mesh or
opposite-coupling-mode axis — use them for structural guidance, but note
that the spec you are given demands a specific mesh (structured hex
20×1×20), a specific coupling pair (FIM benchmark + sequential smoke), and
specific time-step / tolerance values that do **not** match those variants.
The `mandel_tables/` directory (voxel data for displacement ICs) is also
present and fair game.

**Spec:**

> I am setting up a simulation for Mandel's 2D poroelastic consolidation problem, a classic benchmark coupling solid mechanics and single-phase fluid flow. To keep the setup modular, I need to create a reusable base configuration that defines the physics, geometry, materials, and initial/boundary conditions, and then two separate runner configurations: a fully coupled benchmark case and a sequentially coupled smoke test.
>
> ### Domain and Mesh
> We will model a 2D plane strain problem using a structured 3D domain to represent one-quarter of the sample (relying on symmetry).
> - The macroscopic sample sizes we are modeling dictate a quarter-symmetry domain with dimensions $1.0\text{ m}$ in the x-direction, $0.1\text{ m}$ in the y-direction (effectively the thickness), and $1.0\text{ m}$ in the z-direction.
> - Use an internal mesh generator to create a structured grid of $20 \times 1 \times 20$ linear, 8-node hexahedral brick elements.
> - Place all of these elements in a single cell block (e.g., `cb1`) within a designated domain region.
>
> ### Material Properties
> The porous medium consists of a homogeneous solid frame (shale) and a compressible single-phase fluid (water). Do not apply gravity to the system.
>
> **Solid (Shale):**
> - Porous elastic isotropic mechanics.
> - Solid density: $0.0\text{ kg/m}^3$.
> - Bulk modulus: $66.667\text{ MPa}$ ($6.6667 \times 10^7\text{ Pa}$).
> - Shear modulus: $40.0\text{ MPa}$ ($4.0 \times 10^7\text{ Pa}$).
> - Biot porosity model with a reference porosity of $0.375$ and grain bulk modulus of $1.0 \times 10^{27}\text{ Pa}$.
> - Constant, isotropic permeability of $1.0 \times 10^{-12}\text{ m}^2$.
>
> **Fluid (Water):**
> - Compressible single-phase fluid representation.
> - Base density: $1000\text{ kg/m}^3$ at a reference pressure of $0.0\text{ Pa}$ (reference density multiplier = $1.0$).
> - Viscosity: $0.001\text{ Pa s}$ with a viscosibility of $0.0$ and reference viscosity multiplier = $0.001$.
> - Compressibility: $4.4 \times 10^{-10}\text{ Pa}^{-1}$.
>
> ### Initial Conditions
> Set the entire domain to an undrained initial state:
> - Uniform initial pore pressure: $4934.86\text{ Pa}$.
> - Uniform initial total stress (x, y, and z components): $4934.86\text{ Pa}$.
> - Initial displacement fields are prescribed by analytical tables. The x-displacement is scaled by $1.0$ and populated using a 3D table defined by external voxel data (`mandel_tables/xlin.geos`, `ylin.geos`, `zlin.geos`, and `ux.geos`). The y-displacement is initialized to $0.0$. The z-displacement is scaled by $1.0$ and populated by another 3D table (`mandel_tables/xlin2.geos`, `ylin2.geos`, `zlin2.geos`, and `uz.geos`).
>
> ### Boundary Conditions
> Enforce rigid, impermeable, and plane strain boundaries where appropriate:
> - Plane strain/symmetry plane constraints: Lock normal displacements to zero on the negative-x face ($U_x = 0$), the negative-z face ($U_z = 0$), and both the negative-y and positive-y faces ($U_y = 0$).
> - Drainage: The outer lateral edge (positive-x face) is allowed to drain freely, so set its boundary pressure to $0.0\text{ Pa}$.
> - Loading: Apply a prescribed normal vertical displacement on the top surface (positive-z face). This displacement is multiplied by a scale factor of $-1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ and follows a tabulated time history:
>   - Times: 0, 1.00000000e-04, 1.83298071e-04, 3.35981829e-04, 6.15848211e-04, 1.12883789e-03, 2.06913808e-03, 3.79269019e-03, 6.95192796e-03, 1.27427499e-02, 2.33572147e-02, 4.28133240e-02, 7.84759970e-02, 1.43844989e-01, 2.63665090e-01, 4.83293024e-01, 8.85866790e-01, 1.62377674e+00, 2.97635144e+00, 5.45559478e+00, 1.00000000e+01.
>   - Values: 6.31691864, 6.31770204, 6.31834881, 6.31952093, 6.32162575, 6.32534495, 6.33173922, 6.34226498, 6.35857272, 6.38222902, 6.41503816, 6.45995655, 6.52156122, 6.60644048, 6.72413111, 6.8887422, 7.12175231, 7.45621336, 7.9280595, 8.51003414, 9.03811794.
>
> ### Numerics and History Output Strategy
> The physics should be spatially discretized using first-order finite elements for Lagrangian solid mechanics and a finite volume Two-Point Flux Approximation for the single-phase flow.
>
> For outputs, define continuous collection tasks that gather pore pressure across the element block and total displacement on all nodes for time history purposes. These will be exported to files named `pressure_history` and `displacement_history`.
>
> ---
>
> ### Run Case 1: Fully Implicit Benchmark Simulation
> Include the base setup. This run assesses the analytical benchmark over $10.0\text{ s}$ of physical time using a fully implicit poroelastic coupling.
> - Maintain a constant time step of $0.025\text{ s}$.
> - The poromechanics coupling nonlinear solver should allow a maximum of 2 Newton iterations with a tolerance of $1.0\times10^{-2}$. Set max timestep cuts to 1 and maximum line search cuts to 0. Direct parallel evaluation of the linear system should be disabled.
> - Trigger VTK field outputs at an interval of $0.5\text{ s}$. Write out the pressure and displacement histories at every exact timestep.
>
> ### Run Case 2: Sequential Smoke Test Simulation
> Include the base setup. This case briefly exercises the framework using a sequential coupling strategy up to a max time of $0.1\text{ s}$.
> - Enforce the same forced time step of $0.025\text{ s}$. Let the poromechanics solver run continuously until maximum time.
> - The poromechanics coupling should be set to "Sequential", with subcycling enabled, line search action set to "None", 2 max Newton iterations, and a $1.0\times10^{-2}$ tolerance.
> - For the underlying independent solid and fluid solver steps, set their nonlinear solvers to max 40 iterations and a tolerance of $1.0\times10^{-4}$.
> - Request VTK field data and full simulation restart arrays every $0.05\text{ s}$. Similar to the benchmark, record pressure and displacement history files at every exact timestep.
>
> XML files to create: PoroElastic_Mandel_base.xml, PoroElastic_Mandel_benchmark_fim.xml, PoroElastic_Mandel_smoke_sequential.xml

---

## Task 3 — PKN viscosity-dominated hydraulic fracture (`pknViscosityDominated`)

**Why we picked it.** Coupled solid + single-phase flow + surface-generation
(hydraulic fracturing) is the hardest physics family in GEOS, and the PKN
viscosity-storage-dominated regime is specialized enough that the agent
cannot get there from pattern-matching alone. Vanilla Claude Code scores
**fa0 ≈ 0.01** here (total failure) while the plugin + memory variant scores
≈ 0.76. This task is where we most expect humans to either clearly beat or
clearly lose to the agent — both outcomes are informative.

**Physics coverage.** Hydrofracture + SurfaceGenerator + SolidMechanicsLagrangianFEM +
SinglePhaseFVM coupled solver, biased refined mesh with 3-axis piecewise
refinement, frictionless parallel-plates fracture permeability, aperture
table, piecewise time-step schedule, dual history outputs (fracture-field +
source-only).

**Files you must create** (under `inputs/`):
- `pknViscosityDominated_base.xml`
- `pknViscosityDominated_benchmark.xml`

**Blocked reading list (do NOT open):**

Ground-truth XMLs:
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/hydraulicFracturing/pknViscosityDominated_base.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/hydraulicFracturing/pknViscosityDominated_benchmark.xml`
- `data/GEOS/inputFiles/hydraulicFracturing/pknViscosityDominated_smoke.xml`

Tutorial narrative:
- `data/GEOS/src/docs/sphinx/advancedExamples/validationStudies/hydraulicFracture/pknFracViscosityDominated/Example.rst`

**Not blocked but be aware:** the `hydraulicFracturing/` directory contains
poroelastic PKN variants (e.g., `pknViscosityDominated_poroelastic_base.xml`,
`..._poroelastic_benchmark.xml`, `..._poroelastic_smoke.xml`) and KGD decks
that share a lot of structural DNA with the target. These are not blocked —
the agent sees them. Use them for structure, but note the **spec asks for
the single-phase (not poroelastic) coupling** and has a different mesh,
different toughness, and different time schedule. Also fair game:
`inputFiles/hydraulicFracturing/scripts/pkn/` (post-processing scripts only;
do not read, they may contain analytical solutions that give away the answer).

> ⚠️ **Flag**: The `scripts/pkn/` directory is not in the agent's blocklist
> either, so this is a known leakage risk for both the agent and you. Please
> do not open it; we want to compare apples-to-apples, and if you do we will
> ask you to reset.

**Spec:**

> I am looking to simulate the propagation of a Perkins-Kern-Nordgren (PKN) hydraulic fracture under viscosity-storage-dominated conditions. This is a classic benchmark, so I need to closely match analytical solutions where fracture height is constrained, fluid leak-off is neglected, and the fracture propagates laterally in an impermeable rock matrix.
>
> Please set this up using a conceptual split for reusability: place the general material definitions, initial conditions, boundary conditions, and output requests into a generic "base" configuration, and place the specific domain mesh, solvers, geometry, and execution schedule into a "benchmark" configuration that includes the base file.
>
> **Domain and Mesh**
> Because of the inherent symmetry of a PKN fracture, we will model only a quarter of the actual physical domain to save on computational cost.
> Generate a 3D rectangular computational domain of 400 m x 400 m x 800 m using a structured mesh composed of 8-node hexahedral elements.
> The mesh needs local refinement near the fracture plane. In total, it should have 105 x 105 x 60 elements in the x, y, and z directions, apportioned as follows:
> - X direction: from 0 to 150 m (75 cells), 150 to 200 m (10 cells), and 200 to 400 m (20 cells).
> - Y direction: from 0 to 150 m (75 cells), 150 to 200 m (10 cells), and 200 to 400 m (20 cells).
> - Z direction: from -400 to -100 m (10 cells), -100 to -20 m (10 cells), -20 to 20 m (20 cells), 20 to 100 m (10 cells), and 100 to 400 m (10 cells).
>
> **Geometry and Fracture Setup**
> Define the potential fracture plane (the target interface for eventual rupture) covering x from -500.1 to 500.1 m, y from -500.1 to 10.1 m, and z from -0.1 to 0.1 m. Set this plane to initially act as a separable face with a rupture state allowing fracture propagation.
> Within this plane, initialize a preexisting fracture region extending from x = -4.1 to 2.1 m, y = -4.1 to 6.1 m, and z = -0.1 to 0.1 m, with a default uniform initial aperture of 0.02 mm.
> Additionally, define a fluid injection source region right at the origin, extending from x = -2.1 to 2.1 m, y = -2.1 to 6.1 m, and z = -0.1 to 0.1 m.
>
> **Materials and Rheology**
> The domain consists of a single homogeneous and isotropic solid rock formulation, combined with a single-phase compressible fracturing fluid.
> - Rock matrix: Elastic assumed. Density is 2700 kg/m³, bulk modulus is 20.0 GPa, and shear modulus is 12.0 GPa.
> - Fracture conditions: Very low initial rock toughness of 0.1 MPa·m^(1/2) to ensure viscosity-storage dominance. Use a frictionless contact logic and a parallel plates formulation for permeability. Fluid porosity within the fracture should have a reference value of 1.0 and zero compressibility. Define a hydraulic aperture relation mapping coordinates {-0.01, -0.001, 0.0} to values {0.005 mm, 0.01 mm, 0.02 mm}.
> - Fracturing Fluid (Water): Density of 1000 kg/m³, viscosity of 1.0e-3 Pa·s, and compressibility of 5.0e-12 1/Pa. Initialize the water density field to 1000 kg/m³ in the fracture.
>
> **Conditions and Constraints**
> - Standard roller roller boundary constraints should be placed on all normal faces (x, y, z outer boundaries fixed to zero normal displacement).
> - Inject fluid at the defined source region with a constant mass flux. Since we are modeling a quarter-symmetry domain with an intended volumetric rate of 0.0265 m³/s, the scaled mass injection scale should be set strictly to -6.625 kg/s.
>
> **Numerical Solvers**
> Use a strongly coupled system combining solid mechanics, single-phase fluid flow, and fracture surface generation.
> - The mechanics should employ first-order Lagrangian finite elements, targeting both the generic domain and the fracture. Configure its non-linear solver with a 1.0e-6 tolerance.
> - The fluid flow should be solved using a two-point flux approximation (finite volume) with a mean permeability coefficient of 0.8, targeting just the fracture. The non-linear flow tolerance should be 1.0e-5.
> - Fracture propagation should evaluate stress intensity factors exclusively at nodes.
> - Both solvers use iterative linear solvers (GMRES) configured with tight convergence limits. The master coupled solver loop should allow up to 5 resolves and 5 time-step cuts, using an adaptive GMRES/MGR solver framework with a tight Newton tolerance of 1.0e-4.
>
> **Time Schedule**
> The simulation covers exactly 200.1 seconds of injection. Guide the coupled solver with piecewise constant explicit time step sizes:
> - 0 to 1 s: dt = 0.2000000000001 s
> - 1 to 3 s: dt = 0.5 s
> - 3 to 8 s: dt = 1.0 s
> - 8 to 20 s: dt = 2.0 s
> - 20 to 60 s: dt = 4.0 s
> - 60 to 100 s: dt = 8.0 s
> - 100 to end: dt = 10.0 s
>
> **Data Collection and Output**
> Save 3D spatial field metrics (plot level 3, root name "zeroToughness") periodically every 2.0 seconds.
> We need dedicated time series data sampled every 2.0 seconds to validate against the analytical curves:
> - Track the pressure, element aperture, hydraulic aperture, and element area across the active fracture region. Route these collections directly to an output file explicitly named "pknViscosityDominated_output".
> - Separately, extract just the pressure and hydraulic aperture specifically from the fluid injection "source" segment to track near-wellbore dynamics. Log these into an output file named "hydrofracture_source_curves".
> - Include a restart output feature.
>
> XML files to create: pknViscosityDominated_base.xml, pknViscosityDominated_benchmark.xml

---

## Scoring

We score each deck with XMLTreeSim (`fa0` attr-agnostic tree alignment, the
same metric reported in our paper) against the ground-truth deck. Scores
are in [0, 1]; for calibration, the agent baseline per task is:

| Task | Agent no-plugin baseline fa0 | Agent best (plugin+memory) fa0 |
|---|---:|---:|
| Buckley–Leverett | 0.87 | 0.93 |
| Mandel | 0.60 | 0.97 |
| PKN viscosity-dominated | 0.01 | 0.76 |

These are per-seed means from our PAC-1 / D-008 runs on minimax-m2.7. They
are not targets — they are for our internal calibration of whether the
human-vs-agent delta is signal or noise.

## Selection rationale (for our own records, participants can skip)

Picked from the 17-task test set to span physics families and agent
difficulty:

- **Physics diversity**: compositional multiphase flow (Task 1), coupled
  single-phase poromechanics (Task 2), hydraulic fracturing with surface
  generation (Task 3). Covers the three solver coupling families GEOS uses.
- **Agent difficulty spread**: Task 1 is easy for the agent (strong baseline,
  small plugin lift); Task 2 is medium (big plugin lift, moderate baseline);
  Task 3 is where the plugin rescues the agent from total failure. A human
  baseline on all three lets us place humans on the same axis.
- **Scope fit**: 2–3 XML files each, 37–60-line specs. We excluded
  `TutorialSneddon` (7 output files — too long for a timed study) and the
  `AdvancedExample*` wellbore cluster (4 of 17 tasks are the same wellbore
  solver with one material swap — redundant for human eval).
- **Benchmark recognisability**: all three are textbook-class benchmarks
  (Buckley–Leverett, Mandel, PKN) that geoscience PhD students should
  recognize by name, so we measure GEOS-authoring fluency, not physics
  comprehension.
